1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1615	
1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}

1643	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1644	  \(t_i\).

1653	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and

1237	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1238	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1239	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1240	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1241	
1242	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1243	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1244	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1246	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1247	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1248	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1249	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1250	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	\begin{proof}
1253	
1254	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1256	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1257	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1258	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1259	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1260	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1261	\end{proof}
1262	
1263	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1264	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1265	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1267	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1268	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1269	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1270	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1271	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1272	\end{lemma}
1273	\begin{proof}
1274	
1275	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1277	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1278	\end{proof}

1284	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1285	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1286	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1288	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1289	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1290	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1291	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1292	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1294	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1295	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1296	  % L933--938).
1297	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1298	  % equality
1299	  % $$
1300	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1301	  % $$
1302	  % as $R'$-modules."
1303	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1304	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1305	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1306	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1307	  \[
1308	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1309	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1310	  \]
1311	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1312	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1313	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1314	  hypothesis.
1315	\end{lemma}
1316	
1317	\begin{proof}
1318	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1319	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1320	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1321	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1323	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1324	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1325	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1326	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1327	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1328	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1329	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1330	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1331	
1332	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1333	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1334	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1335	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1336	  dictionaries,
1337	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1338	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1339	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1340	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1341	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1342	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1343	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1344	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1345	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1346	  \[
1347	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1348	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1349	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1350	  \]
1351	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1352	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1353	
1354	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1355	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1356	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1357	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1358	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1359	\end{proof}

1441	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write
1442	  \(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\) for the conjugation (mate) bijection,
1443	  which carries \(\alpha\) to a natural transformation \(R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\) of the right
1444	  adjoints. Then for every object \(c\),
1445	  \[
1446	    (\eta_1)_c \circ \bigl(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha\bigr)_{L_1 c}
1447	      \;=\; (\eta_2)_c \circ R_2(\alpha_c) ,
1448	  \]
1449	  the transposed form of the conjugation relating the two units across the mate of \(\alpha\).
1450	\end{lemma}

1452	\begin{lemma}[Unit of a composite adjunction]
1453	  \label{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib}
1454	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.comp_unit_app}
1455	  \mathlibok
1456	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1457	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : F \dashv G\) (with \(F : C \to D\)) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : H \dashv I\)
1458	  (with \(H : D \to E\)) be composable adjunctions, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\). Then the
1459	  unit of the composite adjunction
1460	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2 : (H \circ F) \dashv (G \circ I)\) is, at every object \(X\),
1461	  \[
1462	    \bigl(\mathrm{adj}_1 . \mathrm{adj}_2\bigr)\text{-unit}_X
1463	      \;=\; (\eta_1)_X \circ G\bigl((\eta_2)_{F X}\bigr).
1464	  \]
1465	\end{lemma}

1467	\begin{lemma}[The pullback--pushforward conjugate sends the pullback coherence to the pushforward coherence]
1468	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv}
1470	  \mathlibok
1471	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1472	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be composable morphisms of schemes, and write
1473	  \((h^{*} \dashv h_{*})\) for the pullback--pushforward adjunction on \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
1474	  attached to a morphism \(h\). Under the conjugation (mate) bijection between the composite
1475	  adjunction \((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*})\) and the adjunction
1476	  \(\bigl((f\circ g)^{*} \dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\), the inverse pullback-composition coherence
1477	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is carried to the pushforward-composition
1478	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}\):
1479	  \[
1480	    \operatorname{conj}\!\Bigl((g^{*}\dashv g_{*}) . (f^{*}\dashv f_{*}),\;
1481	      \bigl((f\circ g)^{*}\dashv (f\circ g)_{*}\bigr)\Bigr)
1482	      \bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr)
1483	    \;=\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}.
1484	  \]
1485	\end{lemma}
1486	
1487	\begin{lemma}
1488	
1489	\leanok
1490	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1491	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1493	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1494	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1495	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1496	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1497	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1498	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1499	  composition coherences. Then
1500	  \[
1501	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1502	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1503	    \;=\;
1504	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1505	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1506	  \]
1507	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1508	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1509	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1510	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1511	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1512	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1513	\end{lemma}
1514	\begin{proof}
1515	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1516	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1517	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1518	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1519	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1520	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1521	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1522	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1523	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1524	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1525	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1526	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1527	  the stated equation.
1528	\end{proof}

1530	% --- Seam 2, step (ii): Gamma-collapse of the transparent pushforward coherences ---
1531	% These three project-bespoke atoms record that, on global sections over Spec R, the two
1532	% pushforward-composition coherences and the pushforward-congruence coherence appearing in
1533	% the inner composite are transparent (their section value is the identity / a presheaf
1534	% transport), hence collapse under the global-sections functor.
1535	
1536	\begin{lemma}
1537	
1538	\leanok
1539	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-composition coherence (hom factor)]
1540	  \label{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1541	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1542	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1543	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to \operatorname{Spec} R\) be composable morphisms of
1544	  schemes and \(M\) an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor
1545	  \(\Gamma\) (over the base \(R\)) to the component
1546	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) of the pushforward-composition coherence
1547	  isomorphism yields the identity morphism:
1548	  \[
1549	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\bigr) = \operatorname{id}.
1550	  \]
1551	  The pushforward-composition coherence is transparent at the level of sections.
1552	\end{lemma}

1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}
1770	
1771	\begin{lemma}
1772	
1773	\leanok
1774	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1775	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1777	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1778	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1779	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1780	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1781	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1782	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1783	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1784	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1785	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1786	\end{lemma}
1787	
1788	\begin{lemma}
1789	
1790	\leanok
1791	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1792	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1794	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1795	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1796	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1797	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1798	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1799	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1800	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1801	  \[
1802	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1803	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1804	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1805	      \;\in\; P' .
1806	  \]
1807	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1808	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1809	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1810	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1811	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1812	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1813	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1814	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1815	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1816	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1817	\end{lemma}
1818	
1819	\begin{lemma}
1820	
1821	\leanok
1822	[Shrinking the numerator preserves finiteness]
1823	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1824	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1825	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1826	    lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib,
1827	    lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib,
1828	    lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
1829	  Let \(N_1 \le N_2\) and \(P'\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1830	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1831	  \(N_2/P'\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1832	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N_1/P'\) is
1833	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1834	  \(N_1 \subseteq N_2\) induces an injective \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear
1835	  map \(N_1/P' \hookrightarrow N_2/P'\); since
1836	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) is Noetherian
1837	  (\cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}), the finite module

1839	  (\cref{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}), so its submodule
1840	  \(N_1/P'\) is finite (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}). This supplies
1841	  the finiteness witness of the kernel constructor
1842	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}).

1844	
1845	\begin{lemma}
1846	
1847	\leanok
1848	[Enlarging the denominator preserves finiteness]
1849	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1851	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1852	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1853	  Let \(N\) and \(P_1 \le P_2\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1854	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient

1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1880	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1881	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1882	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1884	
1885	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1886	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1887	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1888	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1889	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1890	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1892	\end{lemma}
1893	
1894	\begin{proof}
1895	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1919	  \[
1920	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1926	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1927	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1928	
1929	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1937	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1938	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1939	\end{proof}

1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1950	
1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}
1968	
1969	\begin{lemma}
1970	
1971	\leanok
1972	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1976	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1978	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1980	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1981	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1982	\end{lemma}
1983	
1984	\begin{definition}
1985	
1986	\leanok
1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1991	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1992	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2007	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.
2013	\end{definition}

2015	\begin{definition}
2016	
2017	\leanok
2018	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2019	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2021	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2022	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2023	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2024	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2025	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2026	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2027	  length-\(r\) datum
2028	  \[
2029	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2030	  \]
2031	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2032	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2033	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2034	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2035	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2036	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2037	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2038	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2039	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2040	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2041	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2042	\end{definition}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2099	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2100	
2101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2102	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2103	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2106	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2107	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2108	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2109	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2110	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2115	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2116	\end{lemma}
2117	
2118	\begin{lemma}
2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2234	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2235	
2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the

3363	\end{lemma}

3365	\begin{theorem}\leanok
3366	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
3367	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
3368	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
3369	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3370	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib,
3371	        lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections,
3372	        lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3373	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
3374	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
3375	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
3376	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
3377	  % $$
3378	  % \xymatrix{
3379	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
3380	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
3381	  % }
3382	  % $$
3383	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
3384	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
3385	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
3386	  % For any $i \geq 0$
3387	  % \begin{enumerate}
3388	  % \item the base change map of
3389	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
3390	  % is an isomorphism
3391	  % $$
3392	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
3393	  % $$
3394	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
3395	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
3396	  % \end{enumerate}"
3397	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
3398	  \(i = 0\) case.}
3399	  Consider the cartesian square
3400	  \[
3401	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3402	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
3403	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
3404	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
3405	    \end{array}
3406	  \]
3407	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
3408	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
3409	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
3410	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3411	  \[
3412	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3413	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
3414	  \]
3415	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3416	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
3417	  \[
3418	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3419	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3420	  \]
3421	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
3422	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
3423	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
3424	
3425	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
3426	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
3427	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
3428	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
3429	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
3430	  an isomorphism.
3431	\end{theorem}

3436	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).

3459	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)

3464	% $$
3465	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
3466	% $$
3467	% see
3468	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.

3470	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
3471	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
3472	% $$
3473	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3474	% $$

3478	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
3479	% $$

3481	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.

3483	%
3484	% In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
3485	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
3486	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
3487	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
3488	% The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
3489	% can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
3490	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
3491	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
3492	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
3493	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
3494	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
3495	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
3496	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
3497	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
3498	\begin{proof}
3499	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3500	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib,
3501	        lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections,
3502	        lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3503	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3504	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3505	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3506	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3507	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.
3508	
3509	  \medskip
3510	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
3511	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
3512	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3513	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3514	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the module comparison map
3515	  \[
3516	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
3517	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3518	  \]
3519	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
3520	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
3521	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
3522	
3523	  \medskip
3524	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
3525	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
3526	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
3527	  \[
3528	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3529	  \]
3530	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
3531	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
3532	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
3533	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3534	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3535	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
3536	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
3537	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
3538	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
3539	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
3540	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
3541	\end{proof}

3015	% --- (C, step 3) The module-level equivalence and its functor identification ---
3016	
3017	% The two blocks below are the module-category layer assembled on top of the topological
3018	% sheaf-congruence def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr: the step-3 equivalence of categories of sheaves of
3019	% modules (def:over_restrict_equiv) and the step-4 functor identification (lem:over_restrict_functor_iso)
3020	% whose object component is the slice-touching bridge lem:over_restrict_iso below. They are
3021	% project-bespoke infrastructure (no external source).
3022	
3023	\begin{definition}
3024	[The slice-to-geometric module-category equivalence (gap1, C, step 3)]
3025	  \label{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3026	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictEquiv}
3027	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3028	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3029	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3030	  \textit{Module-level lift of the topological bridge C; the step-3 equivalence on which the
3031	  slice-touching isomorphism is built.}
3032	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The category of sheaves of modules over the
3033	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) is
3034	  equivalent to the category \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}\) of sheaves of modules on the open
3035	  subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\):
3036	  \[
3037	    \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\bigr)
3038	      \;\simeq\; U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}.
3039	  \]
3040	  This is the module-category lift of the topological sheaf equivalence
3041	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}.
3042	\end{definition}
3043	\begin{proof}
3044	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3045	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3046	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3047	  Apply the module-sheaf transport \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3048	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}) to the site equivalence
3049	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), whose two legs are
3050	  both continuous (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3051	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}). The transport consumes a pair of mutually inverse
3052	  ring-sheaf comparison morphisms between the sliced structure sheaf
3053	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) and the geometric structure sheaf
3054	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U.\mathrm{toScheme}}\); for these one takes the whiskered (co)unit of the site
3055	  equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) into the structure presheaf, paired with the
3056	  identity. The identity is legal precisely because the geometric structure sheaf is
3057	  the transport of the sliced one across the ring-valued instance of
3058	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr} (this is step~2 of the bridge, which holds by
3059	  construction). Orienting the resulting equivalence so that its functor runs from the slice
3060	  side to the geometric side gives the stated equivalence.
3061	\end{proof}
3062	
3063	\begin{lemma}
3064	[Functor identification of the slice-to-geometric equivalence (gap1, C, step 4)]
3065	  \label{lem:over_restrict_functor_iso}
3066	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictFunctorIso}
3067	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3068	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3069	  \textit{The composite of the slice functor with the step-3 equivalence is the geometric restriction
3070	  functor.}
3071	  With \(X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) as above, the composite of the abstract Grothendieck-slice functor
3072	  \(M \mapsto M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) (pushforward along the identity of the sliced ring sheaf) with the
3073	  functor of the step-3 equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) is naturally isomorphic to the
3074	  geometric restriction functor along the open immersion \(U.\iota\):
3075	  \[
3076	    \bigl(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\mathbf{1}\bigr) \;\circ\; (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}
3077	      \;\cong\; \mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota
3078	  \]
3079	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}).
3080	\end{lemma}
3081	\begin{proof}
3082	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3083	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3084	  Both sides are pushforwards of sheaves of modules along the \emph{same} opens functor. Indeed the
3085	  opens functor of the open immersion factors as
3086	  \[
3087	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3088	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}\;\circ\;\mathrm{Over.forget}\,U
3089	  \]
3090	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), an equality that holds by construction, and
3091	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely
3092	  pushforward along \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). The left-hand composite is pushforward along the
3093	  factored functor on the right. Identify the two via the composition law for module-sheaf
3094	  pushforward (\(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\)) followed by congruence of pushforward along the equal ring
3095	  morphisms (\(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)); the required ring-morphism equality is routine.
3096	\end{proof}

3099	
3100	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3101	[The Grothendieck-slice restriction equals the geometric restriction (gap1, C)]
3102	  \label{lem:over_restrict_iso}
3103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso}
3104	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
3105	    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr,
3106	    lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous, lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous,
3107	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}
3108	  % NOTE (iter-031): RESOLVED and axiom-clean. AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso
3109	  % now exists (#print axioms = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}); gap1 bridge C
3110	  % (steps 1->4) is fully closed. The step-2 geometric ring-sheaf identification collapsed to
3111	  % `rfl` (U.toScheme.ringCatSheaf is definitionally the transport of the sliced sheaf, via
3112	  % toScheme_presheaf_obj/map). The statement IS routed through the step-3 equivalence functor as
3113	  % anticipated -- overRestrictIso : (overRestrictEquiv U).functor.obj (M.over U) ≅
3114	  % (restrictFunctor U.ι).obj M -- so no further signature sharpening is needed. The
3115	  % `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` incantation was NOT needed here;
3116	  % reassess only for the downstream P1 Presentation.map transport if its slice synthesis times out.
3117	  \textit{The one lemma that must touch the Grothendieck slice site; everything downstream of it is
3118	  geometric.}
3119	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The \emph{abstract Grothendieck-slice}
3120	  restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) — an object of the category of sheaves of modules over the
3121	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) —
3122	  is canonically isomorphic to the \emph{geometric} restriction
3123	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\)
3124	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), equivalently to the pullback \(U.\iota^{*} M\)
3125	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), as a sheaf of modules on the small Zariski
3126	  site of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\). The isomorphism is induced by the equivalence
3127	  of sites \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U.\mathrm{toScheme})\) coming from the opens
3128	  functor of the open immersion \(U.\iota\), under which the sliced sheaf of rings
3129	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the structure sheaf of \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\).
3130	  For \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) a basic open, the target category is
3131	  \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\)
3132	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3133	\end{lemma}
3134	\begin{proof}
3135	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3136	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3137	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3138	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
3139	  The isomorphism is built in four steps, ascending from the purely topological layer to the
3140	  module-level statement.
3141	
3142	  \emph{Step 1 (topological layer --- done).} The opens functor of the open immersion
3143	  \(U.\iota\) realizes the slice of the opens site of \(X\) over \(U\) as the opens site of the
3144	  subspace \(U\); on sheaf categories this is the equivalence
3145	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\,A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\,A)\) of
3146	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}, for every target category \(A\). This is the
3147	  topos-theoretic layer, assembled from the cocontinuity/continuity of both legs of
3148	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}).
3149	
3150	  \emph{Step 2 (geometric ring-sheaf identification --- the current obstacle).} Identify the
3151	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) with the structure sheaf
3152	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U}\) of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\), transported across the
3153	  \(\mathrm{RingCat}\)-valued instance of \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}; this is an
3154	  isomorphism of sheaves of rings. The bridge is the factorization of the opens functor of the
3155	  immersion,
3156	  \[
3157	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3158	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}
3159	        \;\circ\; \mathrm{Over.forget}\,U ,
3160	  \]
3161	  under which the structure-sheaf comparison reduces to the standard open-immersion
3162	  structure-sheaf identifications; recall that \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\)
3163	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely pushforward along
3164	  \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). This ring-sheaf identification is the remaining geometric
3165	  obstacle.
3166	
3167	  \emph{Step 3 (lift to modules).} Given the step-2 identification of the underlying ring sheaves
3168	  and the continuity of both legs (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3169	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}), lift the sheaf-of-rings comparison to an
3170	  equivalence of categories of sheaves of modules via
3171	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3172	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}). Under this equivalence the sliced
3173	  module \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the geometric restriction
3174	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\).
3175	
3176	  \emph{Step 4 (compose).} Compose with
3177	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
3178	  to land the isomorphism \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U \cong M.\mathrm{restrict}\,U.\iota \cong
3179	  U.\iota^{*}M\). The two sides live a priori in different module categories, so the literal Lean
3180	  statement of \(\mathrm{overRestrictIso}\) is phrased through the step-3 equivalence functor; for
3181	  \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) the target is \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under
3182	  \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3183	\end{proof}

3185	% --- (C, step 4') The pullback form of the slice-to-geometric isomorphism ---
3186	
3187	\begin{lemma}
3188	[The slice-to-geometric isomorphism in pullback form (gap1, C, step 4')]
3189	  \label{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}
3190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictPullbackIso}
3191	  \uses{lem:over_restrict_iso, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
3192	    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
3193	  \textit{The pullback packaging of the slice-touching bridge; the form consumed by the P1 transport.}
3194	  With \(X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) as above and \(M\) a sheaf of modules on \(X\), the transport of the
3195	  abstract Grothendieck-slice restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) under the step-3 equivalence functor
3196	  (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) is canonically isomorphic to the inverse-image (pullback) of \(M\)
3197	  along the open immersion \(U.\iota\):
3198	  \[
3199	    (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(M.\mathrm{over}\,U)
3200	      \;\cong\; (U.\iota^{*})\,M
3201	  \]

1502	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1503	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1504	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
